figures.caption: Table 3: Accuracy of OLMo-Base (pretrained only) versus OLMo (instruction-tuned) under identical shuffling conditions across three benchmarks (Ret setting), probing whether order-independence originates from pretraining or reasoning-specific fine-tuning. SE bounds follow Table 2.

Table 2: Accuracy under five shuffling granularities, two randomisation baselines (Dtok, Dword), and the reasoning-free condition (Rr), across three models (OLMo, GPT-OSS, DeepSeek) and three benchmarks (AIME 2025, CodeElo, GPQA-Diamond). Two evaluation modes are compared: Gen (free generation) and Ret (retrieval with an answer-extraction prefix). Accuracies are single-run binomial estimates; SE≤±2.9pp for AIME (n = 300), ±2.5pp for CodeElo (n = 408), and ±3.6pp for GPQA-Diamond (n = 198).

Table 1: Transformation processors used in this work. We implement five categories: Shuffling (S) permutes content at multiple granularities; Masking (M) replaces targeted character classes with a mask token; Removal (R) strips content entirely; Noise Injection (Nk) inserts false-answer sentences at k× the true-answer frequency; Randomisation baselines (D) replace tokens or words with random samples. Processors compose sequentially (e.g., Sline ◦ Rα).

Table 4: Accuracy under combinations of five interventions—question removal (Rq), alphabet masking (Mα), digit masking (Mν), answer masking (Mans), and line shuffling (Sline)—on AIME 2025 (Math) with GPT-OSS (Ret), probing which components of a reasoning chain are informational. • indicates the intervention is applied; ◦ otherwise. Rows are grouped by the (Rq, Sline) configuration: A = neither, B = Sline only, C = Rq only, D = both; within each group, sub-IDs 1–5 sweep the remaining three masking interventions. ∆Acc is relative to the baseline row A1 (91.3%). Light gray: ∆Acc < −25%; dark gray: ∆Acc < −60%. SE bounds follow Table 2.
